data.title: Sector Update: Tech Stocks Edge Higher Pre-Bell Friday
data.description:
Technology stocks were edging higher pre-bell Friday as the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund was up 0.8% and the SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF was slightly advancing recently.

Rubrik shares were up more than 6% after the company reported a narrower fiscal Q1 non-GAAP loss as revenue increased during the period.

Broadcom shares were down nearly 3% even after the company reported higher fiscal Q2 non-GAAP earnings and revenue.

DocuSign shares were down more than 18% after the company said it now expects lower full-year billings of $3.285 billion to $3.339 billion, compared with its previous guidance of between $3.300 billion and $3.354 billion announced on March 13.

data.title: Global polymer market synopsis
data.description: AsiaHDPE: This week, HDPE prices down adjusted in Sri Lanka and Bangladesh while quoting flat in other parts of the Asian region. Crude oil prices experienced a significant rise amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and disruptions in supply, reflecting concerns over global economic stability and energy security. The escalation of conflicts and production setbacks have heightened uncertainty in the market, underscoring the delicate balance between geopolitical developments and energy supply dynamics. Offers remained largely restricted as certain producers, particularly in the Middle East, withheld their supplies due to worries about reduced profitability, unpredictability about market trends leading up to the Eid al-Adha festival, and the necessity to keep track of freight expenses. In China, discussions about the domestic polyethylene (PE) import prices typically showed a more moderate and subdued tone. This change was mainly caused by a reduction in import arbitrage margins. The decrease in these margins was mainly due to weaker domestic yuan prices in China, which diminished the overall profitability and appeal of importing PE into the nation. Consequently, purchase interest dampened among importers and traders, resulting in a cautious stance during negotiations. Few international sellers reacted to the subdued market environment by reducing their prices for Chinese buyers to encourage sales. Even with these price cuts, numerous sellers encountered difficulties in finalizing actual transactions, as traders and buyers stayed cautious. This reluctance was additionally complicated by the presence of US-origin shipments being offered at very competitive prices for loading periods in June and July. The competitive pricing of these US-origin shipments made it increasingly challenging for sellers from different regions to obtain firm commitments from Chinese purchasers, adding to the overall softening and wary environment in the PE import market throughout the week. In Southeast Asia, Offers for PE supplies from the Middle East remained mostly stable during the week, yet sellers encountered challenges in increasing transaction volumes. Instability in PE prices along with a lacklustre demand forecast caused buyers to be wary while restocking, while opting for local or regional import resources due to shorter delivery times. A few foreign sellers decreased their offers to buyers in China but found it difficult to finalize firm deals, as some traders offered US-sourced shipments for June-July loading at highly attractive prices. In the PE import market of southeast Asia, there were sporadic conversations about some newly available Middle Eastern products, with the majority of transactions later finalized within the assessment ranges. The unexpected developments regarding the US strategy to impose tariffs globally kept creating uncertainty in the market, particularly for economies like Vietnam and China who are facing elevated tariff rates. Furthermore, participants in the Southeast Asian market were vigilant for updates regarding the reopening of significant production sites in the region, as discussions suggested that such events might occur in the following weeks and months. In Vietnam this week, HDPE sentiment remained the weakest, as numerous suppliers reported a lack of purchasing interest for any of their announced offers. In India, PE prices generally held steady throughout the week due to a scarcity of new spot offers in the market for June shipment material. Domestic supply is expected to rise as a key producer intends to restart operations in early June. This enhanced availability, along with a usual decrease in demand post-summer for items like shrink films and packaging materials utilized in the food and beverage sector, is expected to lessen the appeal of imports soon. Moreover, certain industry players indicated that converter stocks remain low, potentially prompting some short-term replenishment efforts. Demand is likely to be dampened even more during the monsoon season in India, as infrastructure projects may be halted during spells of heavy rainfall, leading to a slowdown in the issue of new tenders and cautious market engagement by participants. This temporary decline may affect the overall economic progress in segments dependent on infrastructure growth. In Sri Lanka, Muted trading activity continued to weigh negatively on prices. In the Pakistan and Bangladesh market, buyers expressed reluctance in committing to a few fresh offers that surfaced from Middle Eastern suppliers towards the end of the week. Since buyers were hesitant to make major purchasing decisions at that moment, they opted to delay their commitments until after the Eid al-Adha festival and the Hajj pilgrimage period. This cautious stance was probably prompted by the timing of these religious occasions, which frequently cause changes in market activity, consumer expenditure, and buyer preferences, leading buyers to postpone significant purchases until the celebrations and religious observances are over. Traders and converters have voiced concerns regarding the negative impact that an early monsoon season may have on activities in the South Asian region. LDPE: This week, Low density polyethylene prices dropped in some parts of Asia. Crude oil prices experienced a significant rise amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and disruptions in supply, reflecting concerns over global economic stability and energy security. The escalation of conflicts and production setbacks have heightened uncertainty in the market, underscoring the delicate balance between geopolitical developments and energy supply dynamics. In China, discussions on LDPE imports slowed as decreasing arbitrage margins lowered optimism. The drop in profitability, caused by lower domestic yuan-denominated prices, diminished the attractiveness of imports. As a result, purchasers and dealers exercised caution during discussions In Southeast Asia, overall purchasing sentiment stayed low as buyers remained passive due to global price fluctuations and uncertainty in trade. In the polyethylene (PE) sector, suppliers from the Middle East actively offered LDPE and HDPE shipments in Southeast Asia. Due to relatively lower US-origin prices in the Far East and Southeast Asia, buyers are carefully re-evaluating Middle Eastern offers. In India, LDPE demand continues to be low because of slow end-product sales and abundant supply. The initial monsoon has sped up price drops, particularly for fractional MFI and LD extrusion grades. Enhanced local supply, coupled with a seasonal decline in demand for shrink films and food packaging after summer, is anticipated to reduce import interest. Nevertheless, some market players observed minimal converter supplies, which might lead to restricted short-term replenishment. In Pakistan, the LDPE market experienced a short rise in restocking efforts, as certain buyers entered after weeks of hesitation due to weak demand patterns in Asia. This last-minute purchase gave a fleeting boost to an otherwise quiet market, with participants observing that the momentum was brief and mainly influenced by urgent inventory requirements instead of a wider change in sentiment. The LDPE market saw a brief upturn in replenishment activities, as some purchasers stepped in following weeks of uncertainty caused by subdued demand trends in Asia. The pickup in buying momentum provided a temporary uplift to an otherwise subdued market, with participants noting that the drive was short-lived and primarily driven by immediate stock necessities rather than a broader shift in sentiment. In Pakistan and Bangladesh, end-of-week offers from Middle Eastern suppliers encountered buyer reluctance. Numerous particioants chose to postpone their buying choices until after the Eid al-Adha festivities and Hajj pilgrimage, favouring a watch-and-wait strategy. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices gained in India and Pakistan, while remaining constant in other parts of Asia. Crude oil prices experienced a significant rise amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and disruptions in supply, reflecting concerns over global economic stability and energy security. The escalation of conflicts and production setbacks have heightened uncertainty in the market, underscoring the delicate balance between geopolitical developments and energy supply dynamics. In China, LLDPE prices have remained steady. In the import sector, traders have continued to exercise caution regarding the acceptance of US-origin shipments, although some are contemplating volumes offered at reduced price points. Downstream converters are reportedly not hopeful of obtaining new orders. Concurrently, producers and traders have been reducing their offers to expedite destocking. Futures prices have continued to reflect last week's declines during the initial three trading days of the week. While the US Court of International Trade's suspension of Trump's reciprocal tariffs provided temporary support for futures on May 29, this effect was short-lived following the US Court of Appeals' reversal of the ruling. New import offers remain scarce, and the few that have surfaced are positioned at the higher end of the pricing spectrum. In light of the current weakness in the RMB market, importers are displaying minimal interest in engaging. In the upcoming week, elevated import expenses are anticipated to keep buyers at bay, likely resulting in a downward shift in the center of gravity of China's USD-denominated polyethylene market. In Southeast Asia, LLDPE prices have stayed consistent. Regional converters and local distributors have begun to restock, anticipating that prices have hit their bottom level. Local sellers raised LLDPE prices this week in the key market of Indonesia, suggesting a potential upward trend and sparking increased purchasing interest among converters. However, the buildup of inventories among local stockists and a slowdown in resin consumption by converters may limit purchasing activity in the weeks ahead. Middle Eastern sources have kept LLDPE offers primarily stable, though sellers have encountered difficulties in boosting transaction volumes. Persistent price fluctuations and low demand have led buyers to be wary, with numerous choosing local or nearby imports because of reduced lead times. Simultaneously, the ambiguity regarding the "reciprocal tariffs" on exports from Southeast Asia to the US has led to persistently reduced demand for PE resin. Converters are not accepting new orders for shipments planned past next month. The 90-day tariff suspension is due to expire on 8 July, but converters looking to meet the arrival deadline have essentially missed the shipping window. In India, LLDPE prices increased due to higher offers from international suppliers, even though demand stayed stable, with spot offers restricted across various grades. Purchasers gained increased power in negotiations because of the presence of alternative local supply options. HMEL's transition to manufacturing metallocene-grade LLDPE is not anticipated to greatly affect general supply levels, as the market still experiences sufficient availability from local and global producers. Demand stayed mostly stable as market participants looked for more offers. Purchasing activity was constrained, with just a few necessity-driven buys noted. The monsoon season is anticipated to reduce downstream demand from sectors like construction and infrastructure, whereas demand from other sectors such as packaging is expected to stay mostly unchanged. The sentiment in the LLDPE market remained largely stable to soft, indicating low buying interest, as most purchases for May deliveries have already been finalized. Purchasers predominantly took a wait-and-see stance, expecting new price indicators for June shipments�especially from suppliers in the Middle East. Seasonal demand is expected to decrease in June, particularly from the food and beverage packaging industry, leading buyers to restrict purchases to urgent needs. Numerous converters have been functioning with limited stock, adopting a prudent approach in light of the sustained market weakness. Nonetheless, a minor uptick in sentiment�backed by China's recent 90-day suspension of tariffs�might encourage some restocking shortly. Suppliers are anticipated to make fresh deal announcements in the upcoming week. In Pakistan, LLDPE prices rose as overseas suppliers raised their offers in anticipation of the Eid al-Adha festival. Nonetheless, the majority of purchasers took a wait-and-see stance and displayed minimal enthusiasm for buying at high price points. The demand for LLDPE film stayed considerably low prior to the enforcement of new packaging regulations, affecting consumer sentiment and buying quantities. Moreover, currency variability and continuous import limitations also played a role in the lethargic market, causing buyers to be reluctant in engaging in high-volume deals. The LLDPE market in Sri Lanka and Bangladesh was reportedly stable amid limited import offers from overseas suppliers. However, it remained unclear whether market participants were accepting the higher price levels ahead of the Eid al-Adha festival. PP: This week, Polypropylene prices quoted higher in parts of the Asian region. Crude oil prices experienced a significant rise amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and disruptions in supply, reflecting concerns over global economic stability and energy security. The escalation of conflicts and production setbacks have heightened uncertainty in the market, underscoring the delicate balance between geopolitical developments and energy supply dynamics. PP prices in China increased in tandem with optimistic upstream energy prices and escalating freight costs. Overall trading activity staying low due to a lack of demand for replenishment from local buyers. Both traders and sellers lowered their prices to boost sales and oversee inventory levels. Discussions regarding the export market were constrained by increasing freight costs, especially on routes from China to Vietnam, which experienced smaller hikes compared to other areas. June-loading cargo imports increased as cargo availability grew due to sluggish consumption in Southeast Asia. PP futures experienced a decline for most of the week but saw a partial recovery after a ruling from an international court. Nevertheless, they declined once more after the US Federal Court upheld the reciprocal tariffs set by the former administration. In Southeast Asia, Polypropylene (PP) prices remained stable as regional providers sought to increase export offers. Nonetheless, their efforts were mostly futile due to two main reasons: a surplus of supply in the market and persistent weak demand from downstream sectors. Although PP supplies from China were still easily accessible, their prices were comparable to or even equal to regional offerings, making them less appealing to buyers in the region. A distinct yet important hurdle has surfaced in the form of uncertainty surrounding the possible enforcement of "reciprocal tariffs" on exports from Southeast Asia to the United States. This uncertainty has impacted demand for PP, as numerous converters�responsible for turning the resin into final products�have been hesitant to place new orders due to the possibility of incurring extra costs or facing the risk of delayed arrival of the shipments in the US market. Despite the temporary halt on the tariffs, the actual opportunity to transport goods from Southeast Asia and meet the necessary arrival deadline in the US has already passed. Consequently, purchasers continue to be careful, which additionally restricts any increase in prices within the PP market. In India, prices for polypropylene (PP) exhibited an increasing trend in certain grades as the market took in new offers, while overall demand remained steady without significant fluctuations. In South Asia, there was a significant rise in the prices offered for cargoes of Chinese origin. This was mainly because of increasing shipping expenses from China, resulting from a surge in the transportation of finished goods to the US after a new pact between the two nations halted tariff increases. Consequently, shipping costs increased, and market players expect them to continue rising as carriers assign additional containers to cater to the increased demand. In the meantime, demand stayed fairly stable, as many buyers postponed significant purchases and participated only in selective, necessity-driven buying. Market sentiment is wary, as numerous participants are holding off on making substantial purchases until further proposals emerge. Anticipating the future, the forthcoming monsoon season is likely to witness softening demand from downstream industries such as construction and infrastructure. Nonetheless, the packaging industry's demand is expected to stay consistent despite the rainfall, offering some backing to the market. In general, the market is dealing with a combination of stable demand and cost pressures that may persist in influencing prices in the upcoming weeks. In the Pakistan and Bangladesh market, buyers showed hesitation in committing to some new offers that emerged from Middle Eastern suppliers late in the week. Due to their reluctance to finalize significant purchases at that time, buyers chose to postpone their commitments until after the Eid al-Adha festival and the Hajj pilgrimage season. This careful approach was likely influenced by the timing of these religious events, which often result in shifts in market activity, consumer spending, and purchasing habits, causing consumers to delay major purchases until the festivities and religious observances conclude. Traders and converters have expressed worries about the adverse effects that an early monsoon season might have on activities in the South Asian region. PS: Despite weaker styrene feedstock values, Polystyrene prices were assessed stable in Asia, this week. Crude oil prices experienced a significant rise amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and disruptions in supply, reflecting concerns over global economic stability and energy security. The escalation of conflicts and production setbacks have heightened uncertainty in the market, underscoring the delicate balance between geopolitical developments and energy supply dynamics. A HIPS manufacturer in China stated that toys and essential goods of everyday use, which are simpler to manufacture, like water bottles, have experienced a minor rebound in demand following the US-China 90-day trade truce. Nonetheless, demand from the home appliance industry remains weak since products in this category usually necessitate a more intricate manufacturing procedure, which is also more labor-intensive. As a result, certain home appliance items might not meet the 90-day tariff truce deadline, while taking into account the ongoing uncertainties surrounding the 90-day truce. ABS: ABS prices quoted lower in the most parts of the Asian region this week. Crude oil prices experienced a significant rise amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and disruptions in supply, reflecting concerns over global economic stability and energy security. The escalation of conflicts and production setbacks have heightened uncertainty in the market, underscoring the delicate balance between geopolitical developments and energy supply dynamics. Asian ABS markets faced a downturn in late May, with prices realigning to correspond with tradable levels seen in the market. Producers in the region conceded that ABS encountered greater pricing pressure than polystyrene, a comparable polymer, prompting some to contemplate lowering ABS prices to stay competitive and boost demand. The market was significantly weak from a demand perspective, as numerous buyers and producers concentrated on decreasing their current stock levels instead of acquiring new supplies. This careful strategy was shaped by the current US-China 90-day tariff pause, which added uncertainty to the market. For certain items, especially basic, easy-to-manufacture products like toys and water bottles, demand displayed minor indications of improvement. Nonetheless, for more intricate home appliance parts, which need extended production durations and frequently entail more advanced supply chains, demand stayed low. Producers voiced worries that these items may not gain from the short-term tariff reprieve, particularly in light of the limited timeframe of the truce and the likelihood of additional trade disturbances afterward. From a raw material standpoint, butadiene, an essential feedstock for ABS manufacturing, experienced price declines, indicating a softer market sentiment. Acrylonitrile prices remained steady, while styrene monomer prices slightly decreased, reflecting an overall negative sentiment throughout the upstream supply chain that supported the weaker ABS market forecast. PVC: This week, PVC prices were left unchanged in the Asian region. Crude oil prices experienced a significant rise amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and disruptions in supply, reflecting concerns over global economic stability and energy security. The escalation of conflicts and production setbacks have heightened uncertainty in the market, underscoring the delicate balance between geopolitical developments and energy supply dynamics. In China, a trader remarked that India's key import market is nearing its seasonal slowdown typically seen during the monsoon, along with the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) deadline on June 24. Chinese manufacturers probably won't be certified by the approaching deadline. Consequently, significant Indian purchasers have avoided Chinese products as the deadline nears. This week, China's FOB export offers for carbide-based and ethylene-based PVC were softer, as Chinese producers sought to redirect their focus toward other areas like Southeast Asia. In Southeast Asia, the demand for PVC in end-use applications remains overshadowed by considerable uncertainties. The main element adding to this uncertain environment is the continued unpredictability of US tariffs, which persist as a potential danger. The likelihood of substantial tariffs being applied to exports of finished PVC products to the US continues to be a pressing issue for both regional producers and consumers. Consequently, purchasers in Southeast Asia are taking a careful stance, choosing to acquire PVC only when necessary instead of increasing stocks, primarily due to the surge in freight charges that further elevate total costs. As a result, local PVC usage has flatlined and does not exhibit obvious indicators of recovery or expansion in the foreseeable future. Market participants stay vigilant regarding the changing trade policies and expenses, which are likely to keep applying downward pressure on demand, thus hindering any significant rebound in the region's PVC market operations In India, The demand in India is experiencing a significant change as Indian consumers are progressively steering clear of procuring materials from China. Rather, they are focusing on replenishing their stock through alternative Asian suppliers, a tactical decision influenced by several elements including worries about supply chain reliability, quality requirements, or geopolitical issues. This shift is especially noticeable in anticipation of the forthcoming monsoon season, a time usually marked by heightened logistical difficulties and interruptions. At the same time, there has been a notable advancement in trade policies in the United States. A US trade court first halted the majority of the tariffs levied by former President Donald Trump on May 28, claiming that these tariffs surpassed presidential power and therefore violated constitutional limits. This decision was temporary, as an appeals court quickly reversed the ruling, restoring the tariffs. This series of legal rulings highlights the persistent legal and political discussions related to trade policies, tariffs, and executive authority in the US, which continue to affect international trade dynamics and market stability. Collectively, these changes indicate a wider trend of altering supply chain approaches in India, along with changing legal and policy environments in the US, both of which are expected to greatly impact global trade dynamics and economic ties in the near future. As the BIS certification nears, purchasers have been staying away from non-certified materials, leading to a marked decrease in the uptake of Chinese shipments, since numerous suppliers still lack approval. Ongoing shipping problems heightened caution, as traders noted delays and cancellations, with shipping companies favouring US-bound shipments from China during the 90-day tariff pause. In the future, India's PVC import demand is expected to decline since the monsoon usually reduces pipe demand in key sectors such as agriculture and construction. In Pakistan and Bangladesh, market activity was clearly restricted and diminished in expectation of major religious celebrations, particularly the Eid holidays occurring from June 6 to June 7, along with the Hajj pilgrimage season. These occasions usually result in a brief pause in market activities as participants get ready for the festivities and religious practices, consequently affecting total trading volumes and market momentum during this time. Sufficient regional stock levels and enhanced pre-monsoon rainfall in eastern Pakistan are likely to reduce demand sentiment in the near term. Furthermore, the upcoming renewal of anti-dumping duties (ADD) in June might restrict new proposals until more information becomes available. Container freight rates within Asia have increased from the prior month due to ships being redirected to the China-US route following a recent readjustment of tariffs. A major northeast Asian manufacturer increased its bids for June deliveries earlier in the week relative to May. The majority of CFR-priced offers to significant import markets in Southeast Asia and India occurred at steady-to-strong prices relative to last month, driven by rising freight expenses. PET: This week, PET prices down adjusted in some parts of the Asian region, while prices remained steady in other parts of Asia. Crude oil prices experienced a significant rise amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and disruptions in supply, reflecting concerns over global economic stability and energy security. The escalation of conflicts and production setbacks have heightened uncertainty in the market, underscoring the delicate balance between geopolitical developments and energy supply dynamics. The recent drop in PET prices can mainly be linked to a mix of factors affecting the overall supply chain and market conditions. The drop in feedstock prices for MEG and PTA has been a major factor in driving prices lower. Moreover, the market has faced weak demand, which further added to the decrease in prices. If the demand for PET products decreases or stays flat, suppliers frequently lower prices to boost sales and offload surplus stock. The combination of decreased feedstock expenses and diminished demand results in a market scenario supporting price cuts. A decrease in polyester production is expected to result in a lower demand for the raw materials and feedstocks needed in the manufacturing process. This drop in demand can create downward pressure on the prices of these raw materials, as suppliers might have to reduce their prices to sell off surplus stock or encourage buying activity. As a result, the complete polyester value chain�from raw material providers to fiber manufacturers and downstream fabric producers�could undergo a phase of price modifications, which may result in overall cost decreases within the sector. This interconnected influence emphasizes how a decline in polyester production can cascade through the supply chain, affecting market dynamics, profitability, and the pricing strategies of different participants engaged in the production and distribution of polyester. In Southeast Asia, exporters noted few inquiries and decelerated momentum during the week. Market sources pointed to a lack of stimulus in boosting market sentiment while voicing concerns over the 30% US tariff which has prompted buyers to steer clear of buying material not deemed essential. Another trader echoed the sentiment, noting that China exports roughly 20%-30% of its textiles and garments to the US, with monthly volumes in 2024 averaging around 1 million�1.2 million mt. The outlook will not improve if the US decides to reinstate tariffs once the 90-day pause ends, the trader added. The offers from China and Southeast Asia were comparatively lower, primarily due to the easing of feedstock costs, which contributed to a reduction in overall pricing. Market sources stated that China exports about 20%-30% of its textiles and garments to the US, with average monthly volumes in 2024 expected to be in the range of 1 million�1.2 million mt. ed to take a strong position in June contract talks, seeking to protect margins by opposing any reductions in freely negotiated contract prices. The urgency arises from the wider financial difficulties confronting Europe's petrochemical sector, with continuous asset evaluations and multiple upstream cracker shutdowns recently revealed or being contemplated. Producers persist in highlighting the significance of sustaining financial stability for European PE activities and guaranteeing long-term supply reliability for purchasers. However, their bargaining stance is still limited by ongoing market oversupply and low demand. This discrepancy was clear in May, as producers could not implement margin increases despite major operational interruptions. Significantly, the late-April power disruptions that resulted in short-term closures at PE facilities in Spain and Portugal had a minimal effect on tightening the market. Those facilities have since resumed normal operations, further strengthening the current oversupply situation. LDPE: This week, LDPE prices rolled over in the European region. European low-density polyethylene (LDPE) spot prices stayed generally stable this week, indicating balanced-to-ample supply fundamentals and limited trading activity. Despite small variations, the market has mostly remained stable, supported by indications of ample supply and a lack of urgency from purchasers. The European polyethylene (PE) market is experiencing significant downward pricing pressure because of a consistent stream of competitively priced imports from the U.S., the Middle East, and Asia. This steady import presence has weakened sellers' capacity to increase prices, particularly in the spot market, where buyers display little interest beyond urgent needs. Overall supply continues to be plentiful, with market players expressing no worries regarding material accessibility. Various sources indicated that converters and distributors possess diverse sourcing alternatives, further strengthening market length. In the meantime, demand has remained mostly unchanged. Although spot inquiries are scarce, contract market activity demonstrates a relatively steady offtake�especially from the agricultural film and flexible packaging sectors. With less than six weeks left in the ongoing 90-day EU�U.S. tariff suspension, numerous traders are avoiding additional imports of HDPE, LDPE, and LLDPE from the U.S. Due to standard transatlantic shipping durations and persistent port congestion at various European hubs, the opportunity to safely receive new shipments before July has pretty much ended. However, U.S.-sourced material�especially HDPE�continues to appear economically appealing on paper, with significant arbitrage opportunities still available. Should a risk appetite be present among opportunistic sellers relying on a formal EU�U.S. trade agreement or another tariff pause extension, some additional volumes might still be organized. Nonetheless, these agreements now entail increased financial and logistical risk. Market sentiment has similarly been influenced by changing factors in U.S. trade policy. Even with legal ambiguity, signals from the White House imply that tariffs may be enforced in some capacity unless a trade agreement is formally inked. Consequently, participants in the European PE market remain wary, with numerous buyers sticking to a necessity-driven approach, steering clear of forward purchases and speculative imports. Given that the fundamental demand continues to be weak and no significant enhancement is anticipated shortly, the European LDPE market could persist in an extended downturn. Numerous value chains�especially in consumer products, construction materials, and industrial packaging�are exhibiting indications of structural deceleration. In this situation, both purchasers and vendors seem to be taking a "wait-and-see" stance, focusing on liquidity and inventory control rather than strategies driven by volume. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE spot prices slipped, while contract prices were left unchanged in the European region. Trading activity in the European polyethylene (PE) market stayed quiet, with prices declining as public holidays occurred throughout the region. Even with the calm market environment, demand remained relatively consistent. Nevertheless, sellers still indicated constrained interest in spot volumes. In the contract sector, converters observed steady demand from downstream industries, especially in agricultural film and packaging. Despite overall demand being low, there was an increase in confidence regarding the procurement of US-origin LLDPE grades, which have mostly been free from the extra 25% tariffs. However, any additional decline in US-EU trade relations might question the ongoing validity of this exemption, possibly diminishing the attractiveness of transatlantic shipments. Certain sellers have stated they are postponing further US LLDPE imports until more clarity develops, despite the attractive arbitrage opportunities still being present. A consistent feedstock pricing situation for June has fostered a feeling of quietude among market players. This has also created expectations that PE spot prices might have established a short-term bottom after significant drops in early May. A recent stabilization of US LLDPE export prices has strengthened this feeling. Although the majority of LLDPE imports from the US are free from the extra 25% tariffs, around 50% of all US LLDPE volumes entering the EU are still affected by these duties. HDPE grades are especially vulnerable, representing over two-thirds of the US PE imports affected by tariffs. With less than six weeks left before the 90-day tariff halt ends, the opportunity to arrange additional US LLDPE imports seems to have ended�attributable to shipping delays and congestion at various European ports. As a result, market participants are steering clear of the risks linked to extra US imports, particularly due to ongoing weak demand in Europe. Nevertheless, arbitrage chances for US-sourced HDPE continue to be significant. If some sellers are ready to take chances�encouraged by the possibility of an EU-US trade deal or a prolongation of the mutual tariff suspension�gradual imports might still occur. PP: This week, PP spot prices declined, while contract prices remained steady in the European region. The polypropylene (PP) market in Europe was subdued this week. Homopolymer injection-grade and copolymer polypropylene spot prices decreased throughout the week, as low demand and competitive imports continued to exert downward pressure on spot pricing. The demand for PP remained low, showing minimal purchasing activity observed in the spot market. At the same time, abundant supply also applied downward pressure on prices. On the supply side, imports of PP, especially homopolymer injection grades, from the Middle East and Asia-Pacific that arrived in Europe in May also added to the supply abundance and pessimism observed in recent weeks. Nonetheless, the market showed minimal interest in these proposals. Borealis announced a Eur100 million investment this week to create a new high melt strength PP production line at its facility in Burghausen, Germany. The upcoming line, expected to commence operations in the latter half of 2026, will increase Borealis' capacity to produce recyclable, high melt strength PP threefold, serving the consumer goods, automotive, and construction sectors. However, the relatively stable feedstock propylene's June monthly contract price (MCP) indicates that a potential short-term price floor could have been achieved in Europe. This has generated hope among certain sellers that a rebound in demand might occur in June, following a period of relatively unsatisfactory demand in May. However, purchasers continue to be cautious about committing to any volumes for June, even when presented with price rollovers, as they are in a wait-and-see stance, seeking more insight into the forecast. The tariff environment continues to be dynamic. Worries about a rise in tariffs surfaced when US President Donald Trump suggested a 50% tariff on all imports from the EU starting 1 June. This stands in contrast to a recent thaw in trade tensions between the US and various nations, particularly China. Declines in stock markets were observed on both sides of the Atlantic, with certain downstream sectors possibly vulnerable to a significant decrease in demand for goods sent to the US, particularly in the automotive sector. In the near term, the unpredictability will pose a challenge for all to prepare for and handle. In the meantime, details about US-China trade talks were scarce. This has brought about a change in geopolitical sentiment, leading many market players to now anticipate that trade agreements will be finalized eventually. However, the question lingers on whether this could be accomplished within the designated 90-day pause, or if an extension might be necessary. This signifies a certain degree of uncertainty for PP plants in China that obtain feedstock propylene from PDH units � which use propane imports from the US. A renewed disruption in that flow resulting from escalating US-China trade tensions could impact the economics of PDH and PP plants in China, though such a scenario appears unlikely at this moment. This was, to some extent, also reflected by PP futures on the Dalian Commodity Exchange declining this week, indicating no specific worries about PP supply security in the near future. The general risk tolerance for engaging in long-haul import arbitrages from the Middle East and Asia-Pacific is still low, considering the relatively tight spreads to European spot prices for immediate delivery. Any declines in spot prices for immediate delivery during this time could eliminate any significant price advantage for buyers or traders exposed to this risk. General confidence in the value chains continues to be low, and a wary attitude remains, which is also impacting buyer purchases. The demand for restocking is constrained, as purchasers opt to acquire items only when necessary. PP has faced pressure in the second quarter, and fundamental weakness is anticipated to continue due to its exposure to the automotive, consumer durables, rigid packaging, and construction sectors. Some participants currently expect minimal to no demand rebound this year, given that financial circumstances remain difficult. Certain anticipations suggest that market conditions in June may mirror those from April, as uncertainties in US-EU trade foster caution and dampen activity. In May, PP contract prices have largely aligned with the decline in May MCP for feedstock propylene. Certain buyers advocated for larger reductions to bridge the disparity with spot prices. However, producers justified their contract margins, as some producers' underlying inventories continue to be low, which has consequently reduced the pressure on them to increase volumes. In cases where base prices were elevated, some sharper adjustments were noted. As discrepancies remain between spot and contract prices, and some instances saw widening in May, buyers may persist in seeking additional reductions in June contract prices, particularly if feedstock prices do not increase or demand does not recover. However, producers might maintain a strong position on the necessity to protect margins, pointing to the tough financial circumstances encountered by the European petrochemical sector. Many European PP plants have announced permanent shutdowns, and reviews of assets are currently in progress. There are now numerous reports indicating that a producer based in Saudi Arabia is considering decreasing its presence in Europe. Information is limited at this moment, and it is uncertain which locations and products could be impacted. PS: This week, PS prices journeyed southward in the European region. The European polystyrene (PS) market saw restricted trading activity and a price decrease, with participants initiating contract talks following a fall in the June styrene contract settlement. This represents the third straight monthly drop in PS contract values in Q2 2025, after a phase of increases in Q1, driven by an upward trend in styrene prices. The ongoing decline in prices in Europe is starting to enhance the region's competitive stance, particularly following a period where buyers increasingly turned to Asia for materials because of better pricing and flexible supply conditions. As domestic prices begin to decline, European manufacturers may be able to recover some lost market share in exports and secure their local customer base. Nonetheless, consistently low demand continues to be a vital element affecting market sentiment. Purchasers remain cautious, and a notable resurgence in buying interest is not expected in the near future. The diminished interest is expected to significantly impact current contract negotiations, particularly in vital sectors like packaging, consumer products, and construction. Moreover, elevated inventory levels throughout a significant portion of the value chain have reduced the urgency to make immediate purchases. As summer vacations near in various regions of Europe, seasonality may further diminish market activity, resulting in lower trading volumes during June and early July. Certain producers may need to contemplate short-term reductions in production or alterations to operating rates if demand remains stagnant, especially to avert additional stock accumulation and uphold price discipline. In conclusion, the European PS market is experiencing a period of price adjustment due to low demand, the influence of competitive imports, and a cautious buyer sentiment. Market participants will monitor feedstock trends and any early indications of demand recovery in the upcoming weeks to inform contract choices and inventory strategies. Market participants pointed to steady demand on a week-on-week basis, without significant alterations. Nonetheless, numerous buyers are staying on the sidelines, taking a wait-and-see stance while expecting additional price drops. In the European polystyrene (PS) market, the contract prices for May have declined, reflecting the movement in upstream styrene. Distribution expenses have similarly decreased, reflecting the trend observed in May. Current production hurdles continue in the upstream styrene industry, including maintenance at a local facility in Gonfreville, upkeep at Ellba, a force majeure event at a Tarragona plant, and the permanent closure of a facility in Maasvlakte planned for October 2025. It is still unclear if there will be any impact on PS market fundamentals right now, with players continuing to observe the situation. An EPS converter indicated they were pursuing a price reduction aligned with the decline in styrene, also highlighting that the decrease in benzene spot prices supported the downward trend in feedstock expenses. These cost-side factors are exerting pressure on PS producers to implement price cuts, even while they manage operational expenses and production margins. Moreover, elevated inventory levels throughout a large part of the value chain have reduced the urgency for immediate purchases. As summer vacations near in various regions of Europe, seasonality may further diminish market activity, resulting in lower trading volumes in June and early July. Barring any unexpected macroeconomic or geopolitical disruptions, this steady market situation is expected to persist in the near term. ABS: This week, ABS spot prices tumbled, while contract prices quoted flat in the European region. Market conditions in the European acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS) market remained lacklustre in early June, as demand continued to be weak because of ongoing underperformance in major downstream industries. The automotive sector, a key end-use market for ABS, experienced a further decline in sentiment and future expectations. Continuing worries about possible tariffs, especially concerning electric vehicle imports from Asia, have reduced investor confidence and disrupted procurement plans. A market participant candidly labeled the automotive scenario as a catastrophe, emphasizing the seriousness of the downturn in activity. The appliance and electronics industries, which are other key factors influencing ABS usage, also demonstrated weak performance, thus restricting the rebound in demand. Numerous converters are still functioning at lower rates, maintaining minimal stock levels due to uncertainty regarding order quantities and price forecasts. Provisional measures in the Korean and UNV1 antidumping duty investigations are anticipated to be implemented this month, while the market waits for the same to be announced officially. These actions have garnered some interest, particularly in light of the increasing examination of imports from Asia. Nevertheless, the mood among market participants is careful, with minimal anticipation for any significant immediate effects. Past antidumping measures in other polymer sectors�like PET and PP�have mostly not succeeded in driving substantial demand recoveries or pricing power. In the present environment, market players are prioritizing inventory balance over speculative purchases. The European ABS supply chain is said to be stable, with no significant production problems, yet abundant availability still impacts sentiment negatively. Feedstock prices (comprising butadiene, acrylonitrile, and styrene) have stayed comparatively stable in the past few weeks, alleviating cost-push pressures and aiding in price stagnation. Compounding the tepid situation are logistical challenges in specific regions of Europe, such as port congestion and container shortages, although their direct effect on the ABS trade flow has been minimal up to this point. Export activity stays low, with weak global demand and currency fluctuations restricting arbitrage possibilities. In conclusion, the European ABS market still encounters challenges due to low demand, economic uncertainty, and limited confidence in the success of trade actions. As players attentively watch the forthcoming antidumping measures for potential changes in import patterns or sentiment, anticipations remain rooted in the overarching context of oversupply and sluggish end-user engagement. PVC: This week, PVC prices remained constant in the European region. The European polyvinyl chloride (PVC) market saw limited activity, with market players initiating early contract discussions for June. Concurrently, multiple traders chose to halt acquisitions of new materials from Asia due to increasing freight worries. After the June ethylene contract price (CP) settlement, focus turned to PVC contract negotiations. Discussions stayed in their initial phases. Producers reaffirmed their plan to enhance margins regardless of ethylene price fluctuations, indicating that current profit margins are not sustainable. If European producers uphold stable pricing in the upcoming months, arbitrage possibilities from Asia are expected to continue. Nonetheless, rising freight costs from Asia are introducing unpredictability to import plans. Multiple traders have paused new orders for now, particularly for deliveries anticipated in August, a time usually marked by a seasonal decline in PVC demand in Europe. Although certain sources mentioned a slight increase in demand during the week, the majority of market participants indicated that there was no significant rise in seasonal demand from the construction industry, which typically fuels PVC usage. On the supply side, availability throughout Europe continued to be plentiful. Freight rates from Asia are not only increasing but also skyrocketing, fuelled by scarce container supply, rerouting caused by geopolitical conflicts, and port congestion at crucial transit locations. This trend is expected to continue exerting pressure on import economics for the near future. The market continues to exercise caution, with the price trend for July anticipated to rely significantly on the recovery of construction demand and freight movements. The PVC market in Europe is still traversing May contract discussions, with downward pricing pressure dominating in all regions. Even with the general lacklustre sentiment, producers indicated that demand has been stronger than expected, providing a somewhat more positive perspective than some buyers. Nonetheless, the overall attitude is cautious. Buyers are demanding larger price cuts, yet numerous local manufacturers in Europe seem optimistic about recovering some profit in May, particularly following months of reduced profitability. Demand from the construction industry remains weak, with uses such as window frames and piping still encountering challenges. Moreover, the surge in imports of finished goods�especially in the vinyl flooring category�is putting pressure on demand for certain domestic manufacturers. A market participant characterized the vinyl flooring market as "saturated," pointing out that consumer adoption is sluggish. The demand for emulsion PVC (e-PVC) continues to be sluggish. The European automotive industry, a major user of e-PVC, remains underperforming. While export levels have provided some comfort to producers, persistent trade conflicts have clouded the forecast. Specifically, e-PVC shipments to the United States are facing difficulties because of a 10% tariff imposed on imports from the European Union. Last Friday, the situation intensified when former President Trump suggested a universal 50% tariff on all European exports to the U.S., starting June 1. Although the move might eventually act as leverage in trade talks, the immediate unpredictability is hindering preparations for numerous European manufacturers. PET: This week, PET prices rolled over in the European region. This week, the European polyethylene terephthalate (PET) spot market experienced steady prices, bolstered by increasing demand for locally sourced material. Market players noted a rise in interest for PET, as buyers sought European sources during the current antidumping inquiry into Vietnamese PET. PET manufacturers are increasing production, mainly due to the inquiry concerning Vietnam. Demand levels have not changed much, but strategies for sourcing by buyers have. With increased offers from Vietnam due to the antidumping investigation and elevated shipping expenses, PET purchasers are proactively looking for different suppliers. Consequently, European manufacturers have ramped up production levels and are relying more on the purified terephthalic acid (PTA) feedstock market to satisfy expected demand. Notwithstanding this activity, total PET demand throughout Europe has stayed consistent. The high preform season in mainland Europe is less pronounced than in earlier years. Nonetheless, the demand in the UK during this seasonal time is more robust compared to nearby regions. Contract prices stayed stable, even with weak downstream demand and careful buying behaviour in the bottle and food packaging industries. Although feedstock prices for PTA and MEG rose, weak consumer confidence and decreased end-user activity hindered any price increases. Adding to this, persistent logistical issues and restricted export operations weakened purchasing interest, leading to a more subdued pricing climate. A robust supply supported by consistent production rates and elevated inventory levels was adequate to satisfy the market's stable yet slight demand. Concurrently, diminished purchasing activity and increased import availability applied marginal downward pressure on prices. Consequently, prices decreased slightly by the month-end, indicating a cautiously managed market situation. USHDPE: This week, HDPE export prices rolled over in the US. US spot export HDPE prices held steady for all grades over the week ending, as market fundamentals exhibited minimal to no considerable change. Regardless of the recent instability in associated global polymer markets, HDPE trading stayed subdued, showing only slight changes in prices. Market participants took a wary approach due to persistent geopolitical and trade-related uncertainties. A major worry was the expected 50% tariffs on US-origin products entering the European Union, set to be implemented on June 1. The imposition has been postponed until July 9, providing short-term relief to exporters and purchasers. This delay, although appreciated, has not resulted in immediate action, as both sellers and buyers remain vigilant about the situation. The ambiguity regarding the ultimate result of the tariff discussions has led numerous participants to avoid making substantial commitments, particularly for long-distance shipments to Europe. Traders are tracking cues of a rebound in downstream demand, which has continued to be weak. Until more clarity is reached, particularly concerning US-EU trade agreements and possible enhancements in downstream offtake, HDPE prices are projected to stay within a specific range shortly. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices quoted flat in the US. US spot export LDPE prices held steady across all grades for the week concluded, with market fundamentals remaining mostly the same. Although pricing remained strong, general market activity was quite subdued. Certain traders observed that US-sourced LDPE quantities originally meant for China were redirected to South Korea because of weak demand in China. Nonetheless, this change has had little effect on supporting prices, with demand in South Korea also staying weak. The rerouting of shipments illustrates the wider difficulties confronting the global LDPE market, such as weak downstream demand, shifts in trade flows, and logistical issues. Even with stable prices, worries continue about increasing stocks in the US, as few export options are unable to take in the existing supply. Unless demand significantly increases in major foreign markets, prices are expected to stay pressured in the upcoming weeks. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE export prices were assessed stable in the US. US-sourced LLDPE quantities originally meant for China have been rerouted to South Korea, enhancing supply availability in the region. This change, along with weak buying interest in China, applied more downward pressure on prices than expected. Nonetheless, in spite of the higher inflow, demand in South Korea stayed weak, with end-users exhibiting little interest in restocking due to cautious sentiment downstream. Consequently, prices remained mostly stable throughout the week, as market players closely monitored changes in trade flow and possible demand rebound in both regions. PP: This week, PP export prices left unchanged in the US. US spot export polypropylene (PP) prices held steady in the past week, with the market reacting to the May settlement of polymer-grade propylene (PGP) and anticipating new PP offers from domestic producers. The PGP contract settled at 38 cents/lb for May, unchanged from April, even with prior predictions of a potential 2-cent decrease because of weak downstream demand. This rollover, nonetheless, was seen by certain participants as an indication of possible demand stabilization or recovery. The PGP contract price is a vital reference point for the US PP market, influencing manufacturers' pricing methods and mirroring the overall sentiment in the monomer and polymer industries. With new PP offers for June yet to be finalized, market players are adopting a vigilant approach, navigating between careful optimism and ongoing demand worries. PS: This week, PS export prices quoted lower in the US. During the week, US export prices for general-purpose polystyrene (GPPS) and high-impact polystyrene (HIPS) fell during the week due to ongoing weak demand. In spite of the weak market conditions, PS producers have declared price hikes of 2�4 cents/lb for June, attributing this to cost-push pressures after a 17 cents/gal increase in the USGC benzene contract price, which settled at 269 cents/gal for June. Meanwhile, Ineos Styrolution announced force majeure on styrene shipments from its Bayport and Texas City plants on June 3, which could restrict styrene supply in the upcoming weeks. Nevertheless, market players are wary, observing that the expected increase in demand usually observed in April and May has not yet occurred. Consequently, hopes for a significant recovery in June are still minimal. PVC: This week, PVC export prices gained in the US. The US PVC market experienced a significant increase in activity throughout the week, bolstered by strong demand from major export regions like Latin America and Africa, alongside supply constraints. Multiple producers either held back on making spot offers or functioned with restricted availability, limiting buyer choices in the export market. Consequently, a few suppliers took advantage of the scarcity to request price hikes for June deliveries. Initially, consumers in specific regions opposed the rising price changes, mentioning worries about affordability and unpredictable future demand. Nonetheless, as supply limitations continued and accessible quantities started to decrease, market resistance slowly eased. By the week's end, at least one key producer had exhausted its available supplies, indicating that purchasers had started to accept the elevated prices�either due to necessity or to obtain materials before anticipated further increases. This change signifies increasing momentum in the US PVC export market, as tighter fundamentals bolster stronger pricing sentiment leading into June. PET: This week, PET prices decreased in the US. US spot import PET activity stayed constrained in the week that ended, as rising ocean freight costs and prolonged lead times from North Asia continued to pose difficulties for buyers. Virgin PET prices decreased by roughly 1 cent/lb due to weak demand. Even with the 90-day tariff freeze between the US and China, the significant increase in container rates has neutralized any short-term advantages, maintaining a subdued market sentiment.

data.title: Gold Steady Even as the Dollar and Yields Jump Following U.S. Jobs Report
data.description:
Gold was mostly steady early on Friday even as the dollar and yields were sharply higher after the United States added more new jobs than expected last month.

Gold for August delivery was last seen up US$1.60 to US$3,376.70 per ounce.

The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported the country added 139,000 new jobs in May, down from 147,000 in April but above the consensus estimate for a rise of 125,000 positions, according to Marketwatch. The unemployment rate held at 4.2%.

The better than expected result follows on a Wednesday report that showed U.S. private-sector higher plunged to 37,000 last month, well below expectations for a rise of 110,000 jobs.

The dollar moved higher following the report, which can pressure commodities priced in the currency. The ICE dollar index was last seen up 0.37 points to 99.1. Treasury yields were sharply higher as well, with the U.S. two-year note last seen up 7.9 basis points to 4.007, while the 10-year note was paying 4.471%, up 7.9 points.

data.title: Russia asks UN agency to help solve question of US fuel at Ukraine nuclear plant
data.description:
Russia asked the U.N. nuclear watchdog on Friday to mediate between Moscow and Washington to resolve the question of what to do with U.S. nuclear fuel stored at a Ukrainian power plant controlled by Russian forces.

Russia wants to restart the idled Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant, which lies near one of the front lines of the war in Ukraine and once generated a fifth of Ukraine's electricity. The fuel question is one of numerous issues standing in the way.

Rafael Grossi, head of the International Atomic Energy Agency, told Reuters this week that restarting it was currently impossible because of the lack of a stable power supply and water for cooling.

Russian nuclear energy chief Alexei Likhachev said after meeting Grossi on Friday that Russia was willing either to use the fuel, supplied by U.S. company Westinghouse, or to remove it entirely and return it to the United States.

Westinghouse and U.S. energy officials had previously raised intellectual property concerns with Russia in connection with the fuel issue, he said in televised comments.

State news agency RIA quoted Grossi as saying the IAEA was willing to mediate.

Russia and Ukraine have each accused the other of shelling the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant, Europe's largest, raising the risk of a grave accident.

Likhachev said Russia had prepared a "comprehensive plan" for the phased commissioning of the plant but it could only be implemented if all military threats were removed.

The facility was occupied by Russia in March 2022, shortly its troops entered Ukraine at the start of the war. It has six reactors, the last of which stopped generating electricity in September 2022.

data.title: Russia responds to Trump-Musk feud with jokes, jibes and job offers
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump-Musk feud being closely watched in Russia
  • 'Don't fight, guys!' says former Russian president
  • State media boss mocks 'Industrial Revolution. Only in reverse'
  • Kremlin says confident Trump will sort out the situation

The feud between Donald Trump and Elon Musk provoked chatter, mockery and amusement among the ruling class in Moscow, where one senior official joked about hosting peace talks and another said Musk should bring his businesses to Russia.

"Elon, don't be upset!" nationalist senator Dmitry Rogozin, who once ran Russia's space programme, wrote on Musk's X social media site. "If you encounter insurmountable problems in the U.S., come to us. Here you will find reliable comrades and complete freedom of technical creativity."

Dmitry Medvedev, a senior security official and former president, posted: "We are ready to facilitate the conclusion of a peace deal between D and E for a reasonable fee and to accept Starlink shares as payment. Don't fight, guys!"

The public feud between the U.S. president and the world's richest man was an easy target for Russian politicians who have a history of gloating over perceived turmoil in Washington.

Margarita Simonyan, one of Russia's most powerful state media executives, mocked it as an example of "modern U.S. political culture" — "Sort of like the English Industrial Revolution. Only in reverse."

Kirill Dmitriev, head of Russia's sovereign wealth fund, who has in the past tried to interest Musk in cooperating with Russia on flights to Mars, asked on X: "Why can't we all just get along?" He then asked Grok, X's AI chatbot, how Musk and Trump could reconcile.

Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov, when asked about the clash, said it was an internal matter for the U.S., though he was confident that Trump would handle it.

"Presidents handle a huge number of different things at the same time, some more and some less important," Peskov said.

Others saw clear benefit for Russia from the feud distracting Washington.

"We can just be glad that they won't have time for us," said Konstantin Malofeyev, a hardline nationalist tycoon, who said it was now "the best time to strike back" against Ukraine.

data.title: 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals and Liberty Star Interviews to Air on the RedChip Small Stocks, Big Money(TM) Show on Bloomberg TV
data.description:
ORLANDO, FL / ACCESS Newswire / June 6, 2025 / RedChip Companies will air interviews with 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals, Inc. and Liberty Star Minerals (OTCQB:LBSR) on the RedChip Small Stocks, Big Money™ show, a sponsored program on Bloomberg TV this Saturday, June 7, at 7 p.m. Eastern Time (ET). Bloomberg TV is available in an estimated 73 million homes across the U.S.

Access the interviews in their entirety at:

  • SXTP:https://www.redchip.com/assets/access/sxtp_access

  • LBSR: https://www.redchip.com/assets/access/lbsr_access

In an exclusive interview, Geoffrey Dow, CEO of 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals, appears on the RedChip Small Stocks Big Money™ show on Bloomberg TV to discuss the Company's mission to combat infectious diseases with innovative, small-molecule therapeutics. Dow highlights the potential of ARAKODA® (tafenoquine), an FDA-approved antimalarial developed with the U.S. Army, and its expanding commercial footprint in the U.S. He also outlines 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals' strategy to repurpose tafenoquine for other serious infectious diseases, including babesiosis-a growing tick-borne illness with tens of thousands of new U.S. cases annually. With two potentially pivotal clinical trials underway, a third study in the final planning stages, and a strong foundation of IP, regulatory experience, and institutional support, 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals is advancing a cost-effective, scalable model for addressing unmet needs in global infectious disease treatment.

Pete O'Heeron, Chairman of Liberty Star, and Liberty Star board member Gerardo King, appear on the RedChip Small Stocks Big Money™ show on Bloomberg TV to highlight the Company's dual-track strategy targeting high-grade gold and large-scale copper-gold mineralization in Arizona. They discuss recent bonanza-grade assay results from the Red Rock Canyon Gold Project, where surface samples have returned values as high as 107.5 g/t gold, and the validation of a porphyry copper system at the Hay Mountain Project through Liberty Star's 2024 drill campaign. With gold prices near all-time highs and copper demand surging amid global electrification, Liberty Star is actively pursuing a joint venture to accelerate project development. The company's experienced leadership and strategic positioning in one of North America's most prolific mining regions underscore its potential to unlock significant value through near- and long-term exploration success.

SXTP and LBSR are clients of RedChip Companies. Please read our full disclosure at https://www.redchip.com/legal/disclosures.

About 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals, Inc.

60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals, Inc., founded in 2010, specializes in developing and marketing new medicines for the treatment and prevention of infectious diseases that affect the lives of millions of people. 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals, Inc. achieved FDA approval of its lead product, ARAKODA® (tafenoquine), for malaria prevention, in 2018. 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals, Inc. also collaborates with prominent research organizations in the U.S., Australia, and Singapore. The 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals, Inc. mission has been supported through in-kind funding from the U.S. Department of Defense and private institutional investors including Knight Therapeutics Inc., a Canadian-based pan-American specialty pharmaceutical company. 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals, Inc. is headquartered in Washington D.C., with a majority-owned subsidiary in Australia. Learn more at www.60degreespharma.com. The statements contained herein may include prospects, statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management's current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties. Actual results, performance or events may differ materially from those expressed or implied in such forward-looking statements.

About Liberty Star

Liberty Star Uranium & Metals Corp. (OTCQB:LBSR), d/b/a Liberty Star Minerals, is an Arizona-based mineral exploration company engaged in the acquisition, exploration, and development of mineral properties in Arizona and the southwest USA. Currently, the company controls properties that are located over what management considers some of North America's richest mineralized regions for copper, gold, silver, molybdenum (moly), and associated metals. The Company's premiere property is the Hay Mountain property (exploration stage) for porphyry copper, gold, moly and other commercially important minerals. Specific targets have been selected to explore for near-surface and deep-seated ore bodies, of which there are numerous analogs nearby. Contiguous with the primary Hay Mountain porphyry exploration target, and part of the overall Hay Mountain property, is an increasingly attractive area of exploration stage gold mineralization denominated Red Rock Canyon. Red Rock Canyon exhibits what we believe are extensive, promising hydrothermal associated gold-bearing structures that are documented in historical public and Company records. View numerous geoscientific reports on our website. The Hay Mountain & Red Rock Canyon properties are in Cochise County (southeast) Arizona, USA.

data.title: Renault Group finalises a strategic partnership with Wandercraft, a leader in next generation robotics
data.description:
• Renault Group announces the closing of the acquisition of a minority investment in Wandercraft, a French company with an undisputed leadership in AI-enabled robotic exoskeletons for people with reduced mobility, currently deploying its technology towards mobile robots for industrial uses.
  • This investment comes together with a partnership agreement to develop a family of next generation robots, based on Wandercraft’s technology, firstly for Renault Group’s own manufacturing operations.
  • The partnership brings Renault Group’s industrial expertise to scale up production of Wandercraft’s exoskeletons and robots. It will also help Wandercraft open new business opportunities in robotics.

BOULOGNE-BILLANCOURT, France, June 06, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Renault Group and Wandercraft announce the closing of a minority investment by Renault Group in Wandercraft, the first company to develop, manufacture and market medical self-balancing exoskeletons. Building on its strong expertise in powerful and reliable exoskeletons, Wandercraft has developed an advanced platform for uses such as manufacturing. Renault Group invests together with Wandercraft’s existing shareholders and new investors.

Through a commercial partnership agreement, Renault Group and Wandercraft aim to ensure the development of Calvin, a family of next generation robots, firstly for industrial uses. Calvin will allow Renault Group to relieve its workers from painful and non-ergonomics tasks, while reducing production time and thus gaining on productivity. At a later stage, this partnership will also include the industrialization of robots and exoskeletons by Renault Group in order to reduce costs through design-to-cost and scaling — two disciplines in which the automotive industry is well recognized. This will help Wandercraft to bring its new exoskeleton Eve on the market and open new business opportunities in robotics.

"This partnership with Wandercraft is a forward-looking move. It will allow us to accelerate on automation and to develop robots for our specific auto industrial use, giving us the opportunity to concentrate our people on more value-adding tasks and alleviate operators from painful and non-ergonomics duties. It will drive productivity through the acceleration of production time and costs’ reduction. In the end, it makes a lot of sense to combine Wandercraft’s unique expertise and technology in exoskeletons and robots, with Renault Group’s strong industrial capacity and design-to-cost know-how to bring the production of robots at scale" said Thierry Charvet, Chief Industry and Quality Officer of Renault Group.

“Renault Group’s investment marks a defining moment for Wandercraft. This partnership will boost our ability to build and scale high-impact, low-cost robotics that improve the everyday lives of real people—whether it’s helping individuals with disabilities walk or supporting industrial workers through automation — on the factory floor, in clinics and at home. We’re proud to combine our technology with Renault Group’s industrial excellence to deliver the next generation of mobile robotics” said Matthieu Masselin, CEO and co-founder of Wandercraft.

RENAULT GROUP PRESS CONTACTRie Yamane +33 6 03 16 35 20rie.yamane@renault.comFrançois Rouget+33 6 23 68 07 88francois.rouget@renault.com
WANDERCRAFT PRESS CONTACTLilly Kofler +1 954-292-8187Lilly.kofler@wandercraft.healthJean-Louis Constanza+33 607 529 895Jean-louis.constanza@wandercraft.health

About Renault Group

Renault Group is at the forefront of a mobility that is reinventing itself. The Group relies on the complementarity of its 4 brands - Renault - Dacia - Alpine and Mobilize - and offers sustainable and innovative mobility solutions to its customers. Established in 114 countries, Renault Group sold 2.265 million vehicles in 2024. It employs more than 98,000 people who embody its Purpose every day, so that mobility brings people closer.

Ready to pursue challenges both on the road and in competition, the Group is committed to an ambitious and value generating transformation focused on the development of new technologies and services, and a new range of even more competitive, balanced, and electrified vehicles. In line with environmental challenges, the Group’s ambition is to achieve carbon neutrality in Europe by 2040.

More information: https://www.renaultgroup.com/en/

About Wandercraft

Wandercraft deploys the most advanced robotics to help humans. Founded in 2012, Wandercraft is globally recognized for its next-generation exoskeleton technology that safely and reliably helps walking-impaired persons to walk again. Wandercraft has more than 30 patents and 100 Atalante exoskeletons operating in hospitals across four continents. The new personal exoskeleton, Eve, will be marketed in a few months to assist quadriparetic persons in their daily lives. Wandercraft’s humanoid exoskeletons collectively take over a million steps each month, fueling a proprietary neural network trained on real-world movement data. This dataset, one of the most advanced in human motion, powers Wandercraft’s next generation of exoskeletons and robots, starting with Calvin 40, developed in 40 days as the first of the Calvin family of humanoid robots. Wandercraft believes that robotics should serve the people who need it most.

More information: https://en.wandercraft.eu

data.title: CANADA FX DEBT - Canadian dollar weakens, benchmark yield climbs
data.description:
The Canadian dollar weakened against the greenback on Friday, and the yield on benchmark government debt climbed.

The loonie was trading 0% lower at C$1.3681 to the greenback, or 73.09 U.S. cents, after trading in a range of 1.3661 to 1.3698.

Canadian government 10-year bond yields (CA10YT=RR) rose 6.5 basis points to 3.32%. The yield on similar U.S. government benchmark debt rose to 4.4698%.

U.S. July crude futures rose 28 cents to $63.65 a barrel on Friday.

data.title: Long Heating and Cooling Launches Premium HVAC Maintenance Plan to Enhance System Reliability and Simplify AC Repair
data.description:
Summary: Long Heating and Cooling introduces the Total Comfort Now membership, offering homeowners scheduled HVAC maintenance, priority service, and reduced fees.

Dothan, Alabama--(Newsfile Corp. - June 6, 2025) - Long Heating and Cooling has launched the Total Comfort Now maintenance membership plan to streamline preventive HVAC care for residential clients. The plan introduces scheduled tune-ups, priority service access, and operational benefits aimed at extending the performance and lifespan of home heating and cooling systems.

Long Heating and Cooling developed the membership in response to increased homeowner demand for structured maintenance options amid shifting weather patterns and heavier HVAC usage. As systems are placed under greater strain, proactive maintenance is becoming a practical necessity for homeowners who want to avoid unplanned breakdowns and improve energy efficiency.

The Total Comfort Now membership provides up to two system inspections per year. Each visit includes a 20-point evaluation to help detect common issues before they develop into more complex problems. The plan also removes the dispatch fee for members, providing an added benefit for those seeking consistent maintenance without additional service charges.

The one-hour appointment windows introduced under this membership reduce wait times and help accommodate clients with busy schedules. In addition, members benefit from faster access to online appointment booking, ensuring they can secure service times that fit within their routines.

By introducing the Total Comfort Now maintenance membership plan, Long Heating and Cooling offers homeowners a structured alternative to on-demand heating and air conditioning repairs. The program is intended to reduce equipment failures and promote consistent system performance through regular inspections and service. It also supports a standardized approach to service delivery by incorporating scheduled maintenance features designed to address recurring homeowner needs.

Long Heating and Cooling continues to assess potential updates to the membership plan. These may include optional services such as energy efficiency assessments and integration with smart home technologies to support evolving customer preferences and system monitoring needs.

The Total Comfort Now membership is currently available for purchase through the company's website.

About Long Heating and Cooling:

Long Heating and Cooling provides heating, ventilation, and air conditioning services for residential and commercial clients. The company offers system installations, repairs, and preventive maintenance programs designed to support consistent indoor comfort and energy-efficient performance. With a focus on service transparency and timely response, Long Heating and Cooling works to deliver reliable solutions for both routine and emergency HVAC needs.
